title: Universal criticality of entropy production in chemical reaction networks
abstract: Stochastic thermodynamics gives universal relations for microscopic entropy production, yet its critical behavior at macroscopic nonequilibrium transitions remains unclassified. We study well-mixed reversible chemical reaction networks in the macroscopic-first limit, where transitions arise as local bifurcations of mass-action dynamics. Using linear-noise formulas, center-manifold normal forms, and Floquet theory, we obtain generic exponents for entropy-production fluctuations and responses at pitchfork, transcritical, saddle-node, and Hopf bifurcations. Beyond this low-order classification, a trajectory-space Cramér-Rao type bound yields the universal scaling inequality $α- 2β\geq 0$. Hence divergent responses require divergent fluctuations, but not conversely, making entropy-production fluctuations a sharper probe of nonequilibrium criticality.
summary: This paper provides a complete classification of how entropy production fluctuations scale near different types of nonequilibrium phase transitions in chemical networks. It establishes a fundamental inequality showing that entropy production fluctuations are a more sensitive probe of criticality than parametric responses. The results are universal for any system undergoing these standard bifurcations.
main_problem: The paper aims to classify the universal critical behavior of entropy production fluctuations and parametric responses during macroscopic nonequilibrium phase transitions in chemical reaction networks.
main_result: The authors derive generic critical exponents for various bifurcations and establish a universal scaling inequality, alpha - 2*beta >= 0, proving that diverging responses necessitate diverging entropy production fluctuations.
method: The study employs center-manifold theory, normal forms, Floquet theory, and system-size expansions (Van Kampen/Kurtz) alongside a trajectory-space Cramér-Rao type bound.
model_or_system: Well-mixed, reversible chemical reaction networks (CRNs) described by the Chemical Master Equation and mass-action kinetics in the thermodynamic limit.
key_observables: Variance of entropy production (Var sigma) and the parametric response of the entropy production rate to a control parameter (partial theta sigma).
important_parameters: Control parameter (theta), system size (Omega), observation time (tau), and bifurcation types (pitchfork, transcritical, saddle-node, and Hopf).
main_assumptions: Reversible reactions, well-mixed/homogeneous system, thermodynamic limit (Omega -> infinity) taken before the long-time limit, and non-degenerate/generic bifurcation coefficients.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the parametric response and variance of entropy production for a Hopf bifurcation, while Figure 2 illustrates scaling for a transcritical bifurcation; Table I summarizes critical exponents for all analyzed bifurcations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of entropy production criticality, develops a mathematical framework using system-size expansion and center-manifold theory, classifies exponents for various bifurcation types, establishes a universal inequality via information theory, and validates results with numerical simulations of the Brusselator and Schlögl models.
why_interesting: This work provides a universal framework for understanding how dissipation-related quantities fluctuate near critical points, which is highly relevant to the study of stability and phase transitions in open quantum systems and non-equilibrium many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a): Parametric response ∂bσ. The inset shows the time evolution of the concentration y(t) of the chemical species Y at b = 5.69 in the oscillatory phase. The red vertical dotted line indicates bc. A finite gradient at bc is observed, and hence β± = 0−(nonpositive values) are confirmed. (b): The variance Var σ. The inset shows the power-law slope with exponent α+ = 1. To estimate the critical value bc indicated by the red dotted line, we use the ansatz Var σ = Ωyfσ(Ωx(b −bc)), with bc = 5.67, x = 0.40, and y = 0.42.

FIG. 2. Variance of the entropy production in the model (22) as a function of a. The red vertical dotted line marks ac. The inset shows a log-log plot of the region a > ac indicated by the dashed rectangle; only this side is shown because the behavior is nearly symmetric about ac. Since the system size is sufficiently large, no finite-size scaling is performed. The red dashed line in the inset indicates the power-law slope α+ = 2.
